summary: The fixture-harness wiring builder belongs in `tests/common`, not copy-pasted per suite: three copies of tmpdir → `.temper` → `harness.ts` → `vendor_sdk` collapse into one home

edit.description: The declared one home for test scaffolding — its own header (1-4) names "the SDK vendoring used by tests that drive a real `node` subprocess" as the copy-per-suite it exists to end. It carries every ingredient (`tmpdir` 31, `scaffold` 44, `vendor_sdk` 96) and not the builder that composes them into a fixture harness, which is exactly the surface the three copies below each re-derive. Add that builder: a label plus the fixture program text, returning `(harness, into)`. `tests/install.rs`'s `vendor_sdk` calls are a different job — the install verb scaffolds its own `harness.ts` under test, rather than wiring an authored fixture program — and are out of scope.

`wire_sdk_harness_program` (1223) is the original, and the surface the other two copies' doc comments cite by name as the pattern they follow; `wire_sdk_harness` (1217) binds it to this suite's `HARNESS_PROGRAM`. Retire the local body onto the common home — the `HARNESS_PROGRAM` binding stays suite-local, being this suite's fixture rather than scaffolding.

`wire_memberless_harness` (68-78, shifted +7 by a7a8cc1's supporting-doc rows): a verbatim copy of emit.rs's builder — tmpdir, `.temper`, write `harness.ts`, `vendor_sdk` — whose own doc (65-67) still cites `tests/emit.rs`'s `wire_sdk_harness` as the pattern it follows. Call the common builder with `MEMBERLESS_BUILTIN_PROGRAM`.
`wire_waypoint_harness` (141), the third copy (e76ec85), carrying the same doc cite back to emit.rs's builder. Call the common builder with `WAYPOINT_PROGRAM`.

acceptance: One builder composes tmpdir → `.temper` → `harness.ts` → `vendor_sdk`, in `tests/common`, and no suite re-derives it (engineering.md, "One job, one home": shared fixtures and builders live in one home, never copy-pasted per file). Behavior is unchanged, so the three suites' existing tests are the coverage; `cargo test` green.
notes: Found sweeping 74f4e62..ca4e866: e76ec85 added the third copy, citing emit.rs's builder as the pattern it followed rather than the home it should have extended. **Gate re-pointed at f369531**: SKILL-NESTED-REFERENCE-DOCS shipped, but builtin_lock_frozen.rs stays contended — SUPPORTING-DOC-REACH-CLAUSE rewrites its frozen bytes — so that entry now serializes this one, on the one surviving ground. All three copies re-verified live; only builtin_lock_frozen.rs moved (+7). rescoped at b745415.

summary: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` cites a memory doc that now reads four hops: the gate constant and its own source disagree, and the doc sentence cannot settle which counting is temper's

gate.reason: A human probe rules the hop semantics — the doc text cannot. Fetched live at 420da04 (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-16): 'Imported files can recursively import other files, with a maximum depth of four hops.' Both readings survive that sentence: the root's own import is hop 1 and the chain caps at four edges (⇒ 4), or 'imported files ... recursively import' names a recursion four deep ATOP that first import (⇒ 5, today's constant). Only measuring the runtime's actual load depth decides it, the headless-testbed act that produced the `(mention-gate-containment)` evidence — never plan's to guess and never build's to fetch, because guessing here edits a live gate's semantics: a cap too low forges a dead-member finding on a member that loads, a cap too high suppresses a real one (intent.md invariant 2).

edit.description: The constant and the cite that justifies it. `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` (62) sits under a doc (58-62) asserting 'the `at-import` grammar is recursion-capped at five hops (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-02)' — a number the live page does not say. temper's own indexing IS decidable on disk and needs no ruling: `live_members` (484-523) seeds every own-live member and runs `for _ in 0..MAX_IMPORT_HOPS` BFS rounds, one import edge per round, so the constant counts EDGES traversed from a live seed. What no reading of the disk settles is which edges the doc's 'four hops' counts — hence the park. Whatever the ruling: the cite is re-retrieved, re-dated, and re-worded to state the hop-counting it encodes, never left asserting a bare number its source does not carry. The two narration cites deferring to it — 382-383 and 481-483, both 'as the format documents' / 'the `at-import` recursion depth the format documents' — carry the same claim and move with it.

`the_import_closure_is_hop_capped` (1126) already pins the boundary mechanically rather than in a comment — `r1..r5` rescued from a live seed, `r6` dead — so it encodes today's 5 in the one place a flip cannot silently miss, and it is the test that moves if the ruling is four. Its comment (1129-1130) spells 'the format's five-hop import recursion' and carries the same claim as the constant's cite; re-word it to the ruled reading rather than leaving the fixture narrating the retired number.

tests.asserts: the import closure rescues a chain exactly as deep as the ruled cap and no deeper — the boundary hop pinned at the ruled number, in the ruled counting

acceptance: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`, its doc cite, and the two narration cites that defer to it all agree with the live docs, and each states which hop-counting it encodes (spec-system.md, 'Form rules': an external fact carries its source and retrieval date where it is enforced, verified against current docs, never encoded from memory). The boundary hop stays pinned by a test at the ruled number. `cargo test` green.
notes: Found sweeping b745415..420da04. 28ad61f carried a rider to re-verify graph.rs's two memory cites, bumped the verified one (689 → 677) and declined this rather than flip a live gate inside an unrelated entry — the right call, captured in `.flume/friction/`. NOT citation staleness, so it does not ride: staleness rides because the fact holds; here the constant may be wrong. graph.rs untouched through f369531; every cited line re-verified live. scoped at 420da04.

summary: The packaging remainder after the linux-x64+win32-x64 first cut: darwin binaries (Apple notarizing), the plugin/bundle + marketplace.json, and the binary↔npm version lockstep at the v0.1 tag

gate.reason: Human release actions: John's Apple notarizing for the darwin standalone binary + darwin optionalDependency package; the v0.1 launch tag that flips the binary's self-reported crate version (0.1.0) into lockstep with npm's 0.0.x. Channel 3 (plugin/bundle + marketplace.json) is a git repo + marketplace.json + free community web form (verified 2026-07-06, code.claude.com/docs) — no signing creds — but rides the same release-workflow build-out. release.yml (channel 2, linux-x64+win32-x64) + the SDK launcher/optionalDependencies + standalone tag assets shipped 07-11; the standalone-asset step is wired but unexercised until a tag fires.

edit.description: Extend the existing release.yml (linux-x64 + win32-x64 first cut, the darwin deferral stated at lines 7-9) with the darwin matrix targets (aarch64/x86_64-apple-darwin) + an Apple notarization step, and a channel-3 step producing/publishing the `temper bundle` output with its marketplace.json. Distinct from the standalone tag-asset step, already wired (unexercised until a tag).

acceptance: A stranger acquires temper on all three OSes without a Rust toolchain (darwin binary notarized + pinned), or via the plugin/bundle (marketplace.json); the binary self-reports 0.1.0 in lockstep with the v0.1 npm cut (distribution.md: prebuilt binaries install on all three OSes; lockstep engine pin).
notes: Supersedes retired PACKAGING-CHANNELS (John, 07-11): both its file claims were dead — release.yml now exists (its `new` claim fails the refs gate) and the root-package.json launcher was ruled out (the launcher + optionalDependencies live on the SDK, distribution.md 'What ships'). Release infra outside src/; cargo gates can't validate npm/bundle publish. Park re-tested at f369531: no version tag, crate 0.1.0 vs npm 0.0.7, release.yml:7-9 still defers darwin. scoped at b676656.
